field_definitions.uber_status: Uber's own operating status in this country: nationwide | city_only | taxi_only | banned | absent | gray_area
field_definitions.apps: Ranked list of all rideshare apps available, most prominent first, each with its own status field
field_definitions.geographic_limitations: Specific cities/regions where service is available, if not nationwide
field_definitions.airport_pickup: Rules/restrictions for ride-hail pickup at the country's main international airport(s)
field_definitions.local_taxi_cash_requirement: Whether traditional taxis dominate and/or cash is required or preferred
field_definitions.volatile: Boolean - true if legal status is contested, disputed, actively changing, or inconsistently reported between sources
field_definitions.regulatory_alert: Boolean - true for countries with fast-moving 2024-2026 regulatory action (bans, suspensions, launches, litigation)
field_definitions.confidence: high | low - low indicates thin/hard-to-verify data (small islands, conflict zones, etc), flagged for manual verification
field_definitions.traveler_notes: SIM card, payment method, or other practical quirks for travelers
field_definitions.last_verified: Date this record was last checked against sources
field_definitions.apps[].price_tier: cheap | mid | premium | unknown — relative price positioning vs other apps in the SAME country, based on market-positioning signals from research (not live fare data). 'unknown' = hyperlocal app with no pricing signal available; treat as a gap to fill with real fare data, not as free/expensive.
field_definitions.recommended: Object: {app, why, alternative, budget_note}. The single best default pick for this country. 'app' may be null if no rideshare app operates. For volatile/gray_area countries this reflects the most RELIABLE option, not necessarily the cheapest or most legally 'proper' one — see 'why' for the tradeoff.

advice_thresholds.absent_or_banned: If Uber shows no service, switch to the regional/local champion app (e.g. DiDi in China, Yandex Go in Russia, Snapp in Iran, Grab in most of SE Asia, local taxi app in the Bahamas).
advice_thresholds.taxi_only: Expect regulated taxi-meter fares, not discounted gig-economy pricing. Budget accordingly; the app is a dispatch convenience, not a price advantage.
advice_thresholds.gray_area: The app likely works in practice but may be barred from some airport pickup zones or contested by local taxi unions. Confirm airport-specific rules before relying on it, and consider sitting in the front seat as locals often do to reduce driver friction with authorities.
advice_thresholds.volatile_true: Status has changed recently or is actively disputed. Re-verify within days of travel, not months.
advice_thresholds.confidence_low: Data is thin or hard to verify (small islands, conflict zones, isolated states). Treat as directional, not definitive; a local taxi backup is recommended.

countries.geographic_limitations: Casablanca, Rabat, Marrakech (Uber); broader for inDrive
countries.other_notes: Ministry of Transport declared app ride-hailing outside the legal framework Oct 2025 (unlicensed transport punishable by fines/imprisonment); Uber nonetheless returned Nov 2025 — contradictory, most-cited volatile case globally. ~77,200 taxis and ~180,000 drivers nationally; 1,500+ professional licenses revoked in 2025.

recommended.why: Uber's legal status is actively contradictory here (Ministry declared app ride-hailing outside the legal framework Oct 2025, then Uber re-entered Casablanca/Rabat/Marrakech weeks later in Nov 2025). inDrive and Careem have the longer, steadier operating track record and aren't caught in the current dispute, making them the more dependable pick right now.
recommended.alternative: Careem (similar reliability, slightly higher fares). If Uber shows service in your city, it works, but treat it as second-tier until the legal situation settles.
recommended.budget_note: inDrive's negotiate-your-fare model is also usually the cheapest of the three.

countries.other_notes: Uber has never formally launched in Honduras. Booking through hotels is advisable.

recommended.why: Uber has never formally launched in Honduras, so inDrive is effectively the only international ride-hailing option.
recommended.alternative: Book through your hotel for added reliability, especially at night.
recommended.budget_note: inDrive's negotiate model helps avoid overcharging.

countries.other_notes: Uber has been effectively blocked since 2015 due to opposition from the taxi sector.

countries.other_notes: Uber's legal status in Costa Rica remains formally unresolved.

recommended.why: Uber's legal status remains formally unresolved, and San José's airport (SJO) actively restricts app pickups in favor of the official taxi monopoly. DiDi carries less regulatory baggage for in-city rides.
recommended.budget_note: Book airport transfers through your hotel rather than an app to avoid the SJO taxi-monopoly friction.

countries.airport_pickup: JUTA holds an effective taxi monopoly at airports
countries.other_notes: A year-long rideshare ban had limited real-world effect per the Fair Trading Commission; JUTA taxi union controls formal airport access.

recommended.why: JUTA (the taxi union) holds an effective monopoly at airports and has pushed back hard on ride-hailing; a year-long ban had limited real-world effect per the Fair Trading Commission. inDrive is the most established app option for in-town rides.
recommended.alternative: DoRide, which is JUTA-affiliated and may face less friction.
recommended.budget_note: Negotiate the fare upfront with inDrive; airport transfers are best arranged through your hotel.

apps.name: Bahamas Ride / MyRide
apps.pros: Hails licensed taxis, fixed zone fares

countries.airport_pickup: Lynden Pindling airport taxis (~US$36 to downtown, fixed)
countries.other_notes: No Uber or Lyft. Strong taxi union has historically blocked private rideshare; Uber reportedly in talks as of Feb 2026 but not launched.

recommended.why: There is no Uber or Lyft here — the taxi union has historically blocked private rideshare. This app dispatches licensed taxis at fixed zone fares, which is the closest thing to an app-based option.
recommended.alternative: Flag a taxi directly at a hotel or taxi stand; fares are fixed by zone regardless.
recommended.budget_note: Fares are fixed, not competitive — there's no cheaper alternative to shop for.

apps.name: Uber
apps.pros: Legally contested, especially in Buenos Aires, but operates widely

countries.airport_pickup: EZE airport has documented taxi-driver conflicts with ride-hail
countries.other_notes: Buenos Aires' top court upheld Uber's legality in a 2020 ruling, but friction with taxi unions continues.

recommended.why: Uber is legal per a 2020 Buenos Aires court ruling but still draws friction with taxi unions, especially at Ezeiza airport. Cabify has a longer uncontested operating history in Argentina.
recommended.alternative: Uber is fine for city rides; just expect airport pickup friction.
recommended.budget_note: DiDi tends to run cheaper than both when available.

countries.other_notes: Operates in a largely unregulated gray zone.

recommended.why: Uber is the most widely used option, but this country's ride-hailing legality is in a gray zone — confirm current status and airport rules before relying on it.
recommended.budget_note: Yango tends to run cheaper if available.

apps.name: Uber
apps.pros: Operates via a car-rental legal loophole (UberX/UberYa)

countries.other_notes: Colombia's Supreme Court (2023) held that Uber's use of technology alone isn't unfair competition, but a clear regulatory framework remains pending — a persistent legal gray zone.

recommended.why: Uber operates only via a car-rental legal loophole (UberX/UberYa) that has been challenged in court repeatedly, even though the 2023 Supreme Court ruling was favorable. DiDi and inDrive operate with less legal ambiguity and are widely used locally.
recommended.budget_note: inDrive is typically cheapest; Uber is not necessarily cheaper despite the gray-area status.

apps.pros: Disputed presence; functions despite economic crisis

countries.other_notes: No formal fare regulation; drivers and riders negotiate directly amid hyperinflation.

recommended.alternative: Yummy or RideryApp, which are homegrown and well-established locally.
recommended.budget_note: Always confirm the fare (in USD) before the ride starts, regardless of app.

price_tier_methodology_note: Price tiers and recommendations in this dataset are derived from qualitative market-positioning research (app pros/cons notes, competitive comparisons found in sources), NOT from live or historical fare data. They indicate relative positioning within a country (e.g. 'Bolt is cheap relative to Uber in this market'), not absolute price levels comparable across countries. Recommend treating price_tier as a rough sort signal for MVP, and replacing with real fare-sampling data (e.g. periodic API price checks or user-submitted fares) as a v2 improvement.
